Conclusion

Timberland is clearly a well-run, stable bank. The highest praise one can give to a bank and to a banker is to use the word “boring.” The company is trading within touching distance of my estimate of fair value, with very little upside ahead. Consequently, the most appropriate rating for the bank is a “hold,” especially given the uncertainty around the potential for, and impact of, more aggressive rate cuts.

data.title: Continued Growth Appears To Be On Tap For Natural Gas Services
data.description:
As I mentioned in my first analysis of Natural Gas Services (NGS) in June of this year, I am a big fan of companies that follow an easy-to-understand, yet highly profitable business model. I noted several reasons that support a position in NGS, including its strong profit growth, reasonable valuation, and manageable debt load. Fast forward to December, and the company’s stock has seen a total return of 21.5% in just under six months. The S&P 500 has gained 14.1% during this same time period.

Natural Gas Services released its Q3 2025 financial results on November 10. GAAP earnings of $0.46 per share were 27.8% better than the expected $0.36 and 15% ahead of Q3 2024. The quarter marked the 10th out of the last 11 quarters that the company has topped previous expectations. During its earnings call, the company also raised its earnings guidance for the full year.

These most recent quarterly results have not given me much reason to dampen my enthusiasm for the stock. With natural gas prices rising, the value for the services from NGS will also be more important to companies involved in gas exploration and production, and the company has a strong position with oil companies working in the Permian Basin. I still consider NGS to be a Buy heading into 2026.

Company Overview

Natural Gas Services was founded in 1998 and has its headquarters located in the middle of the oil and gas industry’s heartland – Midland, Texas. Its largest customer is Occidental Petroleum (OXY), which accounted for about 46% of the company’s revenues in the first half of this year. While this concentration of business in one customer certainly opens up NGS to added risks, the company is making improvements to diversify its operations. OXY represented 54% of the company’s revenues in 2024.

NGS provides equipment and services that help oil and gas companies achieve greater yields from their existing wells. They do this through one of the following three ways: leasing out compressors with long-term contracts to oil and gas drillers, selling compressors to those same companies, and servicing existing compressors already in use. The company employs both a sales team and a staff of specialized mechanics, maintaining a network of service centers located in areas with the greatest need for support for oil and natural gas production. However, equipment rental is the company's bread and butter operations. A more complete explanation of the company’s main services can be seen in the slide below from NGS’s Q3 Investor Presentation.

The company has a fleet of large, medium, and small horsepower compressors, with the larger units taking up almost three-fourths of its total rented horsepower. The Permian Basin provides 78% of the company’s total revenues. The next closest exploration site is the Barnett Shale area in Texas, which represents about 7%. About 95% of the business comes from the Southwest, with less than 5% deriving from the Appalachian Basin.

According to the company’s most recent 10-Q, Natural Gas Services controlled $562 million in assets at the end of the third quarter. This is a 14.2% increase from the end of 2024. The bulk of that increase can be attributed to the growth in the company’s rental equipment, which has grown about $64 million, or 15.5%, in the first nine months of this year. As part of that expansion of assets, the company’s long-term debt load has also increased during this time period, rising $38 million from $170 million to $208 million since January 1. Stockholders equity stands at $271 million at the end of Q3, a 6.4% improvement from the start of the year.

When I last wrote about NGS, the company did not pay a dividend. That changed in August of this year when the company issued its first payout, a $0.10 distribution on August 22. In the following quarter, the company announced a 10% increase to that payment as it doled out $0.11 to shareholders on December 3. The company’s Board deemed the increase to be prudent based on its confidence in the outlook for next year. The stock now has a forward yield of 1.43%, which is still below the median for its sector.

Analysis of Natural Gas Services

As part of the company’s most recent financial reporting, CEO Justin Jacobs raised the FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance for the company from a range of $76 to $80 million to a slightly higher spread of $78 to $81 million. Earlier in the year, the projections were for just $74 to $79 million, so the company has been able to exceed its own expectations consistently throughout this year. The EBITDA growth rate for FY 2025 is on track to be about 14.7%.

After completing this year's CapEx spending of $95 to $110 million, the company expects that number to decline in FY 2026. The projected range targeted for next year is just $50 to $70 million. Jacobs stated on the Q3 conference call that he had a “very high degree of confidence” that the final number for next year would be in the lower half of that range. This should allow more profits to potentially fall to the company’s bottom line.

Of all the business lines mentioned earlier in the Company Overview, the rental business is the most important for NGS. A significant majority of shale oil wells in the US need gas compression equipment to make exploration operations profitable. The rental business brings in long-term contracts that have a weighted average of 2.5 years remaining. Monthly fixed fees give the company stable cash inflows, and the company refers to these contracts as “sticky” because once they are on site, they are quite costly to shut down and return to NGS.

As seen in the income chart below, rental revenue has made up 94.8% of the company’s total revenue in the last 12 months. It also provides the company with its highest margin business. The gross margins for the non-rental revenue received over the last year are just 6.1%. However, total gross margins stand at 58.3%, almost 21% higher than they were in FY 2021. Adjusted EBITDA margins are rising consistently as well, reaching 46.6% over the last year at the end of Q3.

In addition to improving margins, one additional positive for the company has been a recent approval for an $11.4 million dollar federal tax refund. The amount is related to net operating losses from 2019 that were identified as part of the CARES Act in 2020. As of November 8, the company announced that it was still waiting on that payment from the federal government, which had been delayed by October’s government shutdown. The total, which breaks down to about $0.90 per share, could be used to pay down debt, buy back stock, or invest in new equipment.

NGS is set to earn around $1.62 per share this year after reporting GAAP earnings of $1.38 in 2024. This year's profit growth of 17.4% is projected to be exceeded in 2026 when the company earns $1.94 for growth rate just under 20%. With a PE multiple of just below 20, the PEG ratio for the company stands at 1, and that is assuming the company only meets expectations, not exceeds them as it has so often in the past.

Risks to Consider

Most of the risks for an investment in Natural Gas Services are the same as they were when my first analysis was published. The business operations for the company depend on the overall health of the oil and gas industry. Oil prices have been on a downward trend since June 2022, but natural gas prices have recently been on an upswing, currently at levels not seen in the US since December of 2022. Changes in the price of crude oil and/ or natural gas, especially sustained decreases in price of both commodities, could impact the demand for compression equipment as extraction of either product becomes less profitable.

Increases in debt levels for NGS could impact future profitability. As of March 31, the leverage ratio for the company was 2.2x. As of September 30, it had risen to 2.6x. Its ratio of enterprise value to expected 2025 EBITDA was 5.5x at the end of Q1. It now stands at 7.2x. Interest expenses for the company reached $9.8 million in the first three quarters of 2025, an increase of about 10.1% from the same period last year. This is something I will continue to watch as I follow the company. NGS has been spending more money on new rental equipment and currently has an 84% utilization rate for its horsepower on hand. It was 82.1% at the end of 2024 so these new assets appear to be serving a greater purpose heading into the final quarter of the year.

When I first wrote about NGS, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating was even more enthusiastic than my own assessment, giving the stock a Strong Buy rating. Today, the opinion of the company seen on Seeking Alpha has dropped to a Hold. The stock receives near-failing marks in Valuation and Profitability. The lowest sub score under Valuation, however, is related to the company’s low dividend payout. The low score in Profitability is assigned because of low levels of cash on the company’s books, even though its gross profit margin and net income margin are above its sector median.

The Bottom Line

Natural Gas Services is not quite the bargain that it was when I first wrote about the company in June. Its PE ratio has climbed up to levels not seen since Q2 2024. Its profit growth has slowed down somewhat this year, even as it is expected to tick back higher in 2026. Nonetheless, the company has shown the ability to continue to expand its operations during a time when the energy sector in the US is benefiting from some much more friendly regulatory conditions. The added bonus of a new dividend, even if it is below average in size, is a welcome enhancement for shareholders. I still believe the stock is a Buy heading into 2026.

data.title: Cognitive Biases And Learning From Past Market Volatility
data.description:
By Catherine Yoshimoto, Director, Product Management, Benchmark Product Development

Market volatility is inevitable - but how investors react to it matters more than the volatility itself. This blog explores how cognitive biases like loss aversion, recency bias, and anchoring have historically pushed investors into costly decisions during downturns and rallies.

Market volatility is often viewed by investors as a threat, but it’s better understood as a defining feature of investing. From the dot-com era to the global financial crisis, the pandemic shock and recent inflation swings, each equity market cycle has tested investor resolve through bouts of volatility.

Reviewing decades of price history in the Russell US Indexes, one truth stands out: how investors respond to volatility often matters more than the volatility itself.

Volatility stirs familiar emotions, including fear and overconfidence. These reactions are natural, but a lack of awareness of our own cognitive biases can lead to costly missteps. Looking back at past cycles reveals practical lessons for navigating the next bout of turbulence with greater discipline and perspective.

Lesson 1: Selling at peak panic can be costly

When markets fall sharply, fear spreads quickly. Loss aversion - the tendency to feel losses more acutely than gains - often drives investors to sell at precisely the wrong time.

During the sharp COVID-19 market downturn in early 2020, for example, the Russell 3000® Index dropped more than 20% in one month, yet it went on to set new highs the following year (see Chart 1). Investors who sold into the panic may have locked in losses and missed the rebound.

Chart 1: Russell 3000 daily price index levels from 31 Dec 2019 to 31 Dec 2021.

Lesson 2: Avoid chasing winners

Just as fear drives panic selling, euphoria can push investors toward riskier behaviour. Rallies can fuel overconfidence and performance-chasing: buying what’s rising instead of what fits a plan. Recency bias reinforces this, as investors assume recent trends will persist.

In early 2021, for example, the Russell 2000 Index soared as post-pandemic reopening enthusiasm drew many investors into small-cap stocks just as momentum was peaking. When inflation accelerated and policy conditions tightened later that year, returns flattened and then reversed through 2022, catching many late entrants into the small-cap market off guard (Chart 2).

A similar pattern emerged more recently with large-cap technology stocks. The so-called “Magnificent Seven” dominated market returns in 2023, lifting growth-oriented benchmarks such as the Russell 1000 Growth Index. Yet by mid-2024, that narrow leadership had begun to fade as valuations moderated and sector performance broadened. In 2025, the earlier pattern has repeated as a few large-cap stocks have dominated the returns of the large-cap index (Chart 3).

Chart 2: Russell 2000 quarterly returns table 2021-2022 (inclusive)

% 3-month Total Return Russell 2000 Total Return USD
2021/03/31 12.70
2021/06/30 4.29
2021/09/30 -4.36
2021/12/31 2.14
2022/03/31 -7.53
2022/06/30 -17.20
2022/09/30 -2.19
2022/12/31 6.23

Source: FTSE Russell, data as of December 31, 2022. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. See the end for important disclosures.

Chart 3: Mag 7 weight in Russell 1000, 31 December 2022-30 September 2025.

It’s often difficult for us to be aware when we have become too optimistic, particularly when the crowd around us is celebrating price gains in the same stocks. That’s why staying grounded in long-term allocation targets and resisting the urge to chase momentum or stretched valuations can help portfolios avoid setbacks.

Lesson 3: Perfect timing is a myth

Even experienced investors fall into the trap of waiting for the “right moment” to invest. This hesitation often stems from anchoring: fixating on a past market level or price to obtain a better entry point. When markets rebound faster than expected, those on the sidelines can miss meaningful gains. Waiting too long can then lead us to “throw in the towel” and panic buy later, missing the bulk of the uptrend and potentially damaging performance if the trend then reverses.

For example, after the sharp decline in early 2020, the Russell 3000 Index regained its pre-pandemic level in less than five months. Investors who were anchored to the idea that prices would “come back down” likely missed the swift rebound. Similar patterns played out after the 2008 global financial crisis and other bear markets, where recoveries began long before sentiment turned positive.

Systematic approaches—such as dollar-cost averaging or regular portfolio contributions—can help investors stay engaged through uncertainty and benefit from compounding over time. In volatile markets, consistency often outperforms precision.

Learning from past market volatility

Sometimes, markets reach such levels of over- or undervaluation that it can take years or even decades for trends to correct. Famously, after the 1929 Wall Street crash, market averages only regained their pre-crash levels during the 1950s. There’s little any investor can do about such major trend reversals.

But this doesn’t mean we shouldn’t apply the lessons of this blog consistently in our day-to-day portfolio management. Every equity cycle teaches patience and perspective. And learning from past reactions can turn volatility from a source of anxiety into a tool for long-term success.

Sources

[1] Sustainable investment asset owner survey 2023

[2] Solving the Scope 3 conundrum (lseg.com)

[3] Results of the 2021 Climate Biennial Exploratory Scenario (CBES) | Bank of England

[4] Investing in the green economy 2023 - entering the next phase of growth (lseg.com)

[5] Mobilising capital for a sustainable global economy (lseg.com)

data.title: Weekly Commentary: $12 TN And Counting
data.description:
Money Market Fund Assets (MMFA) surged $132 billion last week to a record $7.654 TN - the largest increase since the week ended April 1, 2020 (massive covid-related QE). This week saw emerging market (EM) CDS drop five to 130 bps, the low since Q1 2018. What's the source of all this liquidity fueling loose conditions and market complacency?

Global finance is in the throes of history's greatest monetary inflation. Ongoing, unprecedented Credit expansion fuels this inflation. Excessive sovereign debt growth is the most conspicuous offender. Corporate debt markets have been booming. Garnering increasing attention, so-called "private Credit" emerged as a potent force in high-risk lending.

Below the surface - in the shadows yet at the epicenter - a Bubble in speculative leverage evolved into the driving force behind destabilizing liquidity overabundance. The Bubble thesis holds that unprecedented "repo" financing of Treasury and myriad "basis trades" - coupled with global "basis" and "carry trades" - has been the origin of Trillions of liquidity, inflating speculative Bubbles from sovereign debt, equities, AI, crypto, M&A, upper-end real estate, sports franchises, and global asset prices more generally.

The market is pricing a 95% probability for a Fed cut next Wednesday. It's worth noting that money market fund assets have inflated $1.412 TN, or 23%, in 16 months since "The time has come for policy to adjust… We do not seek or welcome further cooling in labor market conditions" - Powell's August 23, 2024, Jackson Hole pronouncement of the end of the Fed's "tightening" cycle.

But the origins of this great monetary inflation go back further. Over three years, money market fund assets inflated an incredible $3.07 TN, or 67%. Back in late-2022, stocks were under significant pressure. The cryptocurrency Bubble appeared to have burst. The Bank of England was forced to counter a major gilt market speculative deleveraging. To sustain its yield curve control (YCC) program, the Bank of Japan purchased a monthly record $128 billion of bonds in December. A few months later, the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank and the resulting panicky bank run saw Fed and GSE assets inflate $244 billion and $352 billion, respectively.

Myriad fragilities had markets convinced the Fed wouldn't dare risk raising rates to the point of interrupting loose conditions. It was a most lucrative bet. "Repo," "basis trade," and speculative leverage began expanding aggressively in late 2022. This speculative Bubble later succumbed to blow-off excess as the Fed and global central community began cutting rates despite highly speculative markets and destabilizing liquidity overabundance.

Over the past 17 weeks, MMFA has inflated $578 billion, or 25% annualized. Anecdotes point to expanding "basis trade" speculative leverage across markets globally. Understandably, there are mounting central banker concerns. Additional insightful commentary (warnings) were provided this week by the Bank of England and Bank of International Settlements.

December 2 - Bloomberg (Greg Ritchie): "The Bank of England warned about growing risks from a profitable fixed-income hedge fund strategy known as the basis trade, calling on market participants to manage their risk taking to avoid a disruptive unwinding of trades that could prompt volatility in gilts. Hedge fund net gilt repo borrowing - where they borrow cash by pledging gilts as collateral - reached close to £100 billion ($132bn) in November…, the highest since data collection began. That compares to a £77 billion estimate in June. The BOE said the rise in borrowing is related to the cash-futures basis trade… The bulk of those positions are driven by entities that are managed outside the UK, with hedge funds managed by US managers accounting for around 60% of the figure. 'The small number of funds running crowded and heavily leveraged trades in the gilt repo market increases the potential risk of sharp moves as funds could need simultaneously to deleverage in response to a shock,' the BOE said."

According to Bank of England estimates, hedge fund "repo" borrowings in the gilt market surged 30% just since June. This parabolic growth is consistent with "repo"/MMFA growth here in the U.S. And it's not by coincidence, as U.S. hedge fund managers are responsible for 60% of the UK's phenomenal "repo" expansion. We can assume that similar aggressive speculative leverage has been employed throughout global markets, explaining what has become systemic liquidity overabundance.

Washington's Office of Financial Research [OFR] was out Thursday with a weighty blog post, "Sizing the U.S. Repo Market" (Ashlyn Cenicola, Melanie Friedrichs, Robert Mann, and Luke M. Olson).

From the blog: "According to new data collected by the OFR, the U.S. repurchase agreement (repo) market averaged about $12.6 trillion in daily exposures in Q3 2025, a number that is about $700 billion larger than previous estimates. The repo market is one of the world's largest and most important short-term funding markets, providing funding for securities dealers and serving as a cash management tool for banks. Despite this fundamental role, this market has historically had limited transparency."

"Of the $12.6 trillion in daily average exposures in Q3 2025, $4.4 trillion was centrally cleared by the Fixed Income Clearing Corporation with another $3.1 trillion settled on Bank of New York Mellon's (BNY's) tri-party platform (excluding centrally cleared). NCCBR [non-centrally cleared bilateral repo] accounted for the remaining $5.0 trillion."

"In Q3 2025, U.S. Treasuries collateralized 88.9% of exposures in the cleared repo segments, but only 61.8% of NCCBR exposures and just over half (52.6%) of the exposures in tri-party."

A "repo" market that has inflated to $12.6 TN. Especially after the 2008 fiasco (not to mention 1994, LTCM in 1998, and March 2020), future historians will struggle to comprehend how speculative leveraging could have been nurtured to such egregious excess. In no way should have an unemployment rate in the low 4% area justified easing conditions with leveraged speculation running wild.

From the OFR blog post, we have confirmation that non-Treasury securities account for a significant amount of "repo" transactions, especially for transactions not centrally cleared (i.e., Mellon's tri-party platform and NCCBR).

OFR data also corroborate the thesis of three years of speculative leverage blowoff dynamics. From September 30th, 2022, to June 30, 2025, hedge fund "repo" borrowings surged $1.972 TN, or 172%, to $3.121 TN. "Prime brokerage" borrowings inflated $1.404 TN, or 88%, to $3.005 TN.

The section "Borrowing by Strategy (U.S. dollars)" features seven hedge fund categories: Credit, Equity, Macro, Multi-Strategy, Event, Relative Value and Other. Notably, since September 2022, Multi-Strategy borrowings have surged $3.3 TN, or 235%, to $4.719 TN. The Equity strategy doubled borrowings over this period to $1.488 TN.

As for size, hedge fund "gross assets" expanded $4.094 TN, or 53%, since Q3 2022 to $11.795 TN, with "gross notional exposure" (incorporating derivatives) inflating $15.146 TN, or 69%, to $37.015 TN. The largest category, Multi-Strategy, saw gross assets expand $1.314 TN, or 60%, to $3.516 TN, with gross notional exposure surging $4.056 TN, or 55%, to $11.379 TN.

Growth in Treasury positions (cash and derivatives) is even more noteworthy. Hedge fund long U.S. Treasury exposures inflated $1.483 TN, or 166%, over 11 quarters to $2.379 TN, while Short U.S. Treasury exposures rose $1.15 TN, or 192%, to $1.748 TN.

Speculative leverage won't balloon forever. De-risking/deleveraging alarm bells were sounding in early-August 2024, with a destabilizing unwind of yen swaps/derivatives. Louder alarms began blaring this past April, with a broadly based fledgling de-leveraging spurring sinking stock prices, along with surging Treasury and global bond yields. The tariff pause rally spurred blowoff levered speculation, with resulting liquidity overabundance masking myriad festering issues.

I can't imagine a more fascinating market backdrop. Stocks are basically at record highs, though trading recently with extraordinary volatility. Bitcoin and crypto under intense deleveraging pressure. A "repo" market demonstrating notable tightness and instability. The yen under pressure, with Japanese government bond yields surging to highs since 2007.

A Friday afternoon Bloomberg headline (Elizabeth Stanton): "US Treasuries Wrap Up Worst Week Since April Amid Fed Doubts." "Yields rose… on Friday to finish a week in which they spiked the most since April, when havoc erupted in global financial markets after the US administration rolled out its tariffs agenda. While traders widely expect the Fed to cut interest rates next week, jitters have emerged over expectations for additional cuts next year."

I'm unconvinced Fed expectations were behind this week's global yield rise. The rates market ended the week pricing almost 100% odds of a cut next week, with the market expecting 58 bps of cuts by the June 17th meeting (down 7bps w-o-w).

German (2.80%), French (3.53%), Greek (3.39%) and Portuguese (3.11%) yields all traded up 11 bps this week. German yields ended the week at the highest level since March. December 3 - Financial Times (Elizabeth Menke and Wendi Carver): "In all the Monday morning quarterbacking of last month's crypto flash crash, one word turns up consistently: leverage. Leading up to the crash, open interest in crypto derivatives had reached an all-time high of roughly $115bn. Within a day, almost $20B in trades were liquidated… While this may not have been a credit event per se, leverage - seemingly everywhere and nowhere at the same time - plays an integral part in the crypto story. Collateralised and unsecured lending in crypto has been around almost as long as the asset itself. After all, what else were you (legally) going to do with it in the early years? It is cited as the weak link that sent the crypto market into a tailspin in mid-2022. Since then, other forms of leverage have taken on greater significance, including margin trading on centralised and decentralised exchanges, options and futures open interest, staking as collateral, perpetual futures…, synthetic leverage, and rehypothecated tokens. Leverage in crypto can be built into smart (ie, programmable) contracts and can arise without explicit borrowing when specific parameters are met. While recent headlines have raised alarms about the borrowing undertaken by crypto treasury companies, at least you can see it on a balance sheet. But much of the leverage in crypto actually sits inside the code and the smart contracts."

After trading down to 83,824 in Monday trading, bitcoin rallied 10,000 (12%) to trade around 94,000 in early Thursday trading. The cryptocurrencies then reversed sharply lower, with bitcoin trading back down to around 88,000 by Friday afternoon. I wouldn't count on a merry crypto holiday season.

I suspect it's early-innings for crypto deleveraging. And it wouldn't take much for de-risking/deleveraging to gain momentum in the levered and crowded AI/tech space. Market operators, though, study the calendar and see a path to locking in strong 2025 gains.

More importantly, global bond markets remain vulnerable. For starters, these are highly levered markets facing endless new supply. Looking at the data, hedge fund leveraging has provided a critical source of demand for heavy government bond issuance in recent years. Rather suddenly, "basis trade" and hedge fund speculative leverage have come under heightened regulatory scrutiny. Pressure will mount on dealers to somewhat tighten "repo" securities finance.

December 2 - Bloomberg (Greg Ritchie and Laura Noonan): "The government-bond trades of the world's largest hedge funds are facing increased regulatory scrutiny, as officials mull policies that would cap the leverage - and profitability - of popular strategies. In separate reports Tuesday, the Bank of England and the… Bank for International Settlements delved into the leverage such funds are accumulating through repurchase agreements, where investors borrow cash by pledging bonds as collateral. Both said borrowing by a small number of large hedge funds poses potential risks to financial stability. The warnings are the latest salvo in a policy debate that could limit the amount of leverage that can be accumulated in so-called repo markets."

What's more, there doesn't appear much left to squeeze out of the global central bank easing cycle. Since September 18th, 2024, the Fed has slashed rates 150 bps. Ten-year Treasury yields closed September 17th, 2024, at 3.65% - 49 bps lower than this week's close.

December 4 - Bloomberg (Ruth Carson): "Kevin Hassett may not have the ability to deliver the rapid pace of interest rate cuts US President Donald Trump would like even if he is approved as the next Federal Reserve Chair, said Gregory Peters, co-chief investment officer at PGIM Fixed Income… 'Does he have the credibility within the committee to drive consensus?' said Peters, who is also a member of the Treasury Borrowing Advisory Committee… 'We don't know that answer. I don't think he has that credibility. I think that's what the bond market is telling you'."

Apparently, we'll have to wait until early in the new year for confirmation that Hassett is the President's Fed guy. And for markets, May is increasingly within the timeframe of concern. For me and others, it's difficult to envisage Kevin Hassett managing a divided committee and operating as the Fed's figurehead (including press conferences) without sounding much the partisan political hack. The confluence of mid-term campaigning and heightened instability will, regrettably, see the Fed knee deep in political muck. Whether it's during Powell's watch or Trump's Chair, serious deleveraging will expose grave system fragilities.

And I chuckle every time I hear Scott Bessent and others discuss shrinking the Fed's balance sheet - "a smaller, simpler Fed." Whether it's Kevin Hassett or not, the administration should be careful what they wish for. Instead, they will oversee a Fed confronting a series of big and complex problems, including markets desperate for liquidity. A surprising surge in global yields would throw sand in a lot of gears.

The… organisation expects the US Federal Reserve to cut interest rates just twice more by the end of 2026, before keeping the federal funds rate at 3.25% to 3.5% throughout 2027."

Emerging Market Watch:

December 5 - Bloomberg (Vinícius Andrade, Daniel Carvalho and Giovanna Bellotti Azevedo): "Brazilian markets slumped, with stocks seeing their worst day since 2021 and the currency tumbling about 2.5% after former President Jair Bolsonaro endorsed one of his sons as the right-wing's name in presidential elections next October. The real… accelerated losses after Flavio Bolsonaro confirmed the news on X. The Ibovespa stock index fell 4.3%, while some swap-rate contracts - indicators of monetary policy expectations - surged more than 50 bps…"

Leveraged Speculation Watch:

December 2 - Bloomberg (Greg Ritchie): "The world's largest hedge funds are accumulating 'substantially higher' leverage in the bond market than smaller rivals thanks to their close relationships with dealers, according to the Bank for International Settlements. The average haircut - a reduction on the value of collateral in a repurchase agreement transaction - applied by banks to the 10 largest hedge funds was close to zero, according to BIS research... Such minimal haircuts allow these funds to obtain 'very high levels of leverage relative to their smaller peers,' it said. The BIS finding is the latest scrutiny into highly-profitable fixed-income strategies such as the basis trade, as regulators seek to tame the risks from such leverage being unwound in a selloff. Earlier Tuesday, the Bank of England called on participants to manage their risk taking to avoid market disruption. 'These large hedge funds are key clients of major dealers, giving rise to strong trading relationships that dealers appear to reward with more attractive haircut terms,' Felix Hermes, Maik Schmeling and Andreas Schrimpf wrote…"

Social, Political, Environmental, Cybersecurity Instability Watch:

December 4 - Axios (Emily Peck): "Young Americans say the country is heading down a dark road and fear their futures are unstable, according to the latest Harvard Youth Poll… Financial insecurity, intense political polarization and the rise of AI are eroding Gen Z's faith in their economic prospects and public institutions overall. The authors of the report… warn that this massive upheaval is threatening the country's stability. 'Instability is shaping nearly every part of young people's lives,' said John Della Volpe, director of polling at the Harvard Institute of Politics… A majority of respondents (57%) say the country is headed in the wrong direction - a six-point rise from last year…"

data.title: Pampa Energia: The Turning Point In Argentina's Most Resilient Energy Player
data.description:
When I published my previous article on Pampa Energía (PAM) in late August, the ADR was trading at around $70, and at that time, my position was buy. The elections were still two months away, and the market was moving blindly, speculating on opposing regulatory scenarios.

In September we saw a more negative climate, especially after the victory of the opposition in the Province of Buenos Aires, a result that was reversed on 26 October, when the victory of Milei generated one of the strongest movements of the year in Argentine energy assets. At that time, the market immediately understood that it had ahead of it at least two years of a more predictable, freer, and less interventionist framework, something that could directly improve the profitability of an integrated player like PAM, to the point that in the pre-market of the day after the election, the stock advanced about 25%.

To explain the PAM trade today, which is around $92, an increase of more than 30% since my previous article, it is not enough to look only at the electoral result, which I consider very favorable.

I also think the market finally began to price a company that had been doing the work in silence for several quarters, and that ended up consolidating that perception with the Q3 results. In this scenario I reaffirm my position and still see Pampa as a buy.

Q3: the quarter where everything was aligned

When analyzing the results of Q3, I see that PAM continues moving in the direction it was already doing in previous quarters, with shale oil growing rapidly, gas holding even when demand behaves very erratically, electricity generation benefiting from deregulation, and capex that does not work as a brake, but as the bridge to a more ambitious production profile.

Without a doubt, Rincón de Aranda is the driver that most changes the story. To put it in context, three new pads in the quarter brought shale oil production to 14,400 bpd, almost triple Q2, and a seventh pad connected after the quarter brought volume to 16,000 bpd.

However, what struck me most were PAM’s production expectations for the next two years, as mentioned in the call:

We expect to exit 2025 producing 20,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day. To support further growth and leverage idle capacity, trumpa capacity in the pipeline, we plan to install an additional temporary facility next year, increasing production to an average of 28,000 barrels per day by the second half of 2026. Our target is 45,000 barrels per day by 2027 once Vaca Muerta oil Sur pipeline and our central processing facility as known as CPF are brought online.

These figures also have to be read in a context where, until recently, PAM was essentially a gas company. Today oil is no longer a secondary activity and is becoming a source of EBITDA, with margins increasing as lifting costs decline. The CFO explained that this is what is happening:

Therefore, we will see a reduction in our lifting cost for oil from $10 to around $9.1, $9.2 per barrel, and that will drive down our overall lifting cost to around $6.2 per barrel equivalent.

For me this line summarizes everything, because it shows a reduction in costs that does not arise from accounting adjustments, but from real scale, supported by volumes that grow steadily.

Although the results of the shale were extraordinary, they were not accompanied by a deterioration in gas; on the contrary, winter allowed the company to demonstrate the technical capacity of PAM: 17.6MMm3/day, the highest value in the history of the company.

Electricity generation also performed well, even with a 9% drop due to maintenance. PEPE6 contributed more than expected, and the progress of deregulation since Milei’s assumption is beginning to be seen, as PAM historically operated with margins "tied" to the regulation in generation. Today the story is different, and the possibility of self-sufficiency in gas begins to change the dynamics of margins.

Finally, capex. Pampa invested $317MM in the quarter, +225% YoY, and still maintained a cash position of $881MM and a net debt of $874MM, with a leverage of 1.3x.

We know that 2025 and 2026 are the most intensive years in terms of investment for Rincón de Aranda and projects associated with generation, as explained in detail by the CFO:

This year is basically what we projected on the budget or slightly lower than what we projected on the budget because of very small delays in the deployment on some payments that are not going to be done this year, but are going to be done early next year. So it’s going to be around $1.1 billion. And next year, we expect roughly to maintain that level of CapEx. That is basically until we finish the ramp-up of Rincón de Aranda because -- as we said, Rincón de Aranda takes 70%, 75% of that number.

Valuation and peers: why PAM remains cheap even after rising 30%?

Every time I go back to analyze the valuation of PAM I find myself facing the same dilemma, since it is an integrated company that lives in several worlds at once. It’s not just upstream, it’s not just gas, and it’s not just generation. Even so, I see that there are numbers that repeat and show that the stock continues with a discount difficult to justify.

Today PAM is trading at an EV/EBITDA FWD close to 6x, which implies a discount of around 40% compared to the sector average, and the P/E FWD is around 14x, around 25% below the sector. This is especially striking for a company projecting EPS growth far above the industry.

To put it in context, if I look at Vista (VIST), which is the most visible peer in unconventional Argentine oil, I see that it operates with an EV/EBITDA close to 4.8x, and YPF (YPF) is even slightly above that, around 4.8x as well.

But we must also bear in mind that the comparison is not entirely fair, as PAM has lower risk, less exposure to upstream regulation, and greater diversification. I also note that PAM now has more accelerated growth, as VIST is now entering a more moderate stage after the strong growth of the previous two years, while, as shown in Q3, PAM is just entering its phase of accelerated expansion.

Growing, however, I see that the gap is even more marked. PAM projects a revenue increase of over 50% YoY for the next twelve months, while the sector is in a low digit. The FWD EBITDA grows around 15%, above average, and the EBIT is practically multiplying compared to the figures of the sector. The EPS has an estimated increase of more than 100%, a magnitude that allows me to explain why I consider it difficult to maintain the current multiples.

In profitability, PAM also stands out. The ROA is close to 5%, about twice the industry average, and the net margin is around 17%, above 12% of the sector. However, I also see a squeezed operating margin, but this is explained by what we analyzed previously, a context of high capex, in the transition to shale oil. With lifting costs down to the PAM estimate of $6/boe and production that will expand strongly over the next 24 months, we can expect an improvement in efficiency.

Risks

After reviewing the quarter and valuation, I am also clear that the risks exist and are not minor. First of all, despite the results favorable to the current administration, we must remember that Argentina remains a volatile country, even in a more liberalized context like the one we have today.

On the other hand, the projected capex for 2025 and 2026 is demanding, which forces PAM to have a very precise execution, since the speed at which it invests does not leave too much margin of error.

At the same time, PAM remains exposed to the Brent movement, although the use of hedges helps moderate extremes. We must also consider that the Chilean market, which today provides a significant volume of gas exports, can vary according to conditions that change every year, so it is not appropriate to assume that this flow will be totally stable.

Conclusion

When I look at the quarter’s results in the current macroeconomic context, where the Milei government will seek to advance market-oriented reforms, I see PAM entering a phase that is clearly different. Today shale oil is reconfiguring its EBITDA structure at a speed that a year ago seemed unlikely to me, gas continues to show stability, and electricity generation finally finds a regulatory framework that allows it to capture more margin.

And all this happens while the stock continues to trade with multiples that do not reflect the magnitude or the speed of growth projected for the next two years. That is why, as in August, I maintain my position: Buy.

data.title: The Trade Desk: A Generational Buying Opportunity Emerges (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in April of this year, I initiated coverage on The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) with a technically based piece. The stock did gain for a period after my article, but after pulling back significantly, losses now total ~20% since publication. With consumer confidence seemingly shaky, it's actually not a major surprise that there has been considerable weakness, as the willingness to pay for ads may be under some pressure at the moment. With the company reporting earnings back in November, today let's see if the current stock price presents an opportunity for investors.

In the below analysis, it is determined that not only is there opportunity in the stock, there is likely currently a generational opportunity. While revenue growth slowed again for Q3 and guidance for Q4 was softer than desired, the long-term growth story remains unshaken. The company is a leader in this space and has the track record to prove it. Furthermore, the digital ads market continues to expand, and so if you look past the near term consumer confidence headwinds, the rock-bottom level in the valuation is a major buying opportunity. In my previous article, I mainly based my bearishness off of the technicals, but after reviewing the company's fundamentals today, I believe The Trade Desk is a strong buy at current levels.

Big Picture Should Calm Investors

Above is a timeline of the company that's in their 2025 Q3 earnings presentation. I'm not going to get into the details of the numbers. Instead, I believe this infographic provides a good big picture sense of where things are really at. Sure, 2025 has been a year to forget in terms of stock performance, but really it's more than likely that temporary ad spending headwinds will eventually dissipate. The progress they have made throughout the years speaks to their commitment to growth, and so while they are hitting some speed bumps now, odds are that the company will bounce back.

Above, we discussed that history shows the company's internals are strong. Well, let's take a look forward. Despite consumer weakness potentially weighing on ad spend now, the long-term trend is that the digital ad market is only getting bigger. The market is truly enormous at $935 billion+, and so the potential for growth is immense as the industry as a whole continues to grow. When combining the strong internals of the company with the long-term trends of market expansion, I would say that the outlook for The Trade Desk is quite clearly a bright one. Now, let's turn towards how they performed in Q3.

Growth Slows For 2nd Straight Quarter

For 2025 Q3, The Trade Desk reported total revenues of $739 billion, representing YoY growth of 18%. While these figures are really respectable in isolation, note that the growth rate has actually hit multiyear lows for two consecutive quarters. Back in Q2, there was a top line increase of 19%, and so there has been a modest deceleration for Q3. It is more than likely that consumer weakness is a driver of this relative sluggishness. However, as mentioned earlier, this will eventually pass.

Despite the lowest growth in years, their revenues were still able to beat analyst expectations by $20.09 million, and so results are better than feared. Furthermore, the company reported that they had customer retention of over 95% in the quarter. They also stated that this metric has been this high for 11 consecutive years, and so it is obvious that their platform remains the go-to for ad buyers. This is another source of reassurance that there is nothing specifically wrong with the company's value proposition.

EBITDA Margin Expands

Firstly, for the bottom line, The Trade Desk reported an adjusted EPS of $0.45, up 9.76% YoY. While that is actually a rebound from the previous quarter's sluggish growth rate, this increase still lags the top line growth rate as an indication of shaky profitability. However, this may be a bit misleading regarding the true efficiency of the business.

The company likes to use adjusted EBITDA as one of their core metrics, and I agree that this figure usually tells the better story vis-a-vis the actual profitability of a company's operations. For adjusted EBITDA, they reported $317 million, up 23% YoY. That outpaces the revenue growth rate. As a result, adjusted EBITDA margin expanded from 41% to 43% as a demonstration of operational improvement. Therefore, while the company can't control the macro-environment, they continue to make internal improvements that count.

Share Buybacks Are Aggressive

In Q3, The Trade Desk generated operating cash flows of $224.69 million, which is a little disappointing, as that represents a drop YoY. After subtracting CAPEX and whatnot, FCFs came out to being $155 million, and that also represents a notable drop from the year ago quarter. Personally, this isn't too concerning for me, as the long-term outlook remains strong, as emphasized many times already. What I really want to discuss in this section is their aggressive share buyback policy.

In the quarter, The Trade Desk bought back $310 million worth of Class A common stock. That left $60 million in remaining share repurchase authorization, and they used it all in October. Firstly, note that they bought back far more stock than they had FCFs in the quarter. In fact, the $310 million is more than FCF for any quarter in the past three years, and so it is likely they see their stock as significantly undervalued. It seems that they are tapping into their cash reserves, and this can be seen as a statement of confidence regarding future cash flows. Looking forward, the board has already approved another $500 million for their share repurchase program, and so shareholders should be encouraged by what they see.

Near Term Outlook

For the fourth quarter, The Trade Desk sees revenues of at least $840 million. That means that they're expecting top line growth of at least 13% YoY. Since there was an 18% revenue increase in Q3, there is a good likelihood that there will be a third straight quarter of deceleration, and that probably played a part in spooking investors.

As for adjusted EBITDA, they are projecting a figure of $375 million, and that implies a YoY growth rate of 7%. That lags the projected top line growth rate, and so the strong operating profitability we saw in Q3 is not expected to continue.

Overall, I would say that Q4 guidance is indeed weaker than desired. While the slowdown in revenue growth is not too surprising with consumer confidence still under pressure, I would say the weak adjusted EBITDA estimate is a bit disappointing.

Still, this is just a near-term outlook, and I view the long term growth story as still intact despite these speed bumps. Furthermore, you can see in the EPS chart from back in the adjusted EBITDA discussion that EPS growth is expected to bounce back as we head into 2026 and so Q4 weakness will likely be the exception rather than the norm even in the near future.

Valuation Hits Multiyear Low

As you can see above, the forward P/E ratio for The Trade Desk has collapsed this year. After a brief rebound during the middle of the year, the multiple now finds itself at fresh multiyear lows. I believe this potentially creates a generational buying opportunity.

In today's article, we have learned that the company is indeed facing some near-term headwinds. Revenue growth is slowing, and despite strong EBITDA margin in Q3, there will be profitability weakness in Q4. However, I believe this is really all just noise. The key takeaway is that the company has a track record of growth, and the long term outlook for digital ads is as bright as ever. Near-term consumer headwinds are here and now, but they won't last forever. Once the environment improves, I believe The Trade Desk is very well positioned to bounce right back.

There is a risk that the consumer slump will last longer than expected, with the direction of inflation still being uncertain, but sooner or later, the wind will be back in the company's sails. Therefore, I believe the multiyear low in the forward P/E ratio really creates a prime entry opportunity for investors.

Final Thoughts

A generational buying opportunity usually involves a fundamentally robust business selling for a major relative discount as a result of temporary headwinds causing investor panic. I believe this sums up the situation that The Trade Desk is in right now. Especially when considering that the company is now aggressively innovating in AI to minimize disruption potential, the outlook for the business is strong, as near-term headwinds are unable to reverse positive long term trends for digital ads. As stated at the beginning, my previous article on The Trade Desk was rooted in the technicals, but after looking at the fundamental setup today, I believe the stock is a strong buy currently.
